content.content: {"body":" **Toggle button strings not localized in onboarding view** The toggle button text here is hardcoded in English while the equivalent button in `page-providers.js` correctly uses `t()`. The locale keys `providers:showAllModels` and `providers:showFewerModels` were added to all three locale files (en, fr, zh) in this PR but are never consumed in the onboarding view, so French and Chinese users will see raw English text. ```suggestion >${showAllModels ? t("providers:showFewerModels") : t("providers:showAllModels", { count: hiddenModelCount })}` : null} ``` Compare with `page-providers.js` line 304 which does the same thing correctly: ```js t("providers:showFewerModels") : t("providers:showAllModels", { count: hiddenCount }) ```","id":3024454033,"in_reply_to_id":null,"line":916,"path":"crates/web/src/assets/js/onboarding-view.js"} {"body":" **JSDoc example for Anthropic models is incorrect** The comment claims `"claude-opus-4-6-20260301" → 4.6`, but the implementation extracts the **maximum** numeric token, which for that string is `20260301` (the date suffix), not `4.6`. The function still produces a correct *relative ordering* within a single provider (higher date = higher score), but the documented return value is wrong, which can mislead future contributors. ```suggestion * Extract the highest version number from a model ID for sorting. * e.g. "gpt-5.4-mini" → 5.4, "claude-opus-4-6-20260301" → 20260301, "o4-mini" → 4 * For models with a date suffix the date itself becomes the sort key, which is * intentional — newer dates rank higher. Returns 0 when no number is found. ```","id":3024454110,"in_reply_to_id":null,"line":8,"path":"crates/web/src/assets/js/helpers.js"}

input.description: Get full body of new review comment
Get full body of the new comment

content.content: **Hardcoded timestamps will cause test failures** `list_includes_created_at_in_response` uses absolute timestamps for two models that are then queried through `service.list()`, which now applies the new 1-year legacy filter: - `1_770_000_000` ≈ **Feb 2026** → `gpt-5.3` will be filtered out around **February 2027** - `1_760_000_000` ≈ **Oct 2025** → `babbage-002` will be filtered out around **October 2026** When `babbage-002` ages past the cutoff, both `assert_eq!(arr.len(), 3)` (line 11313) and the `.find(...).unwrap()` for `babbage-002` will panic. The `gpt-5.3` lookups fail roughly four months later. The new tests `list_hides_legacy_models_by_default` correctly avoid this by computing timestamps relative to `SystemTime::now()`. The same approach should be used here: ```rust // Instead of hardcoded absolute timestamps: let recent_gpt = std::time::SystemTime::now() .duration_since(std::time::UNIX_EPOCH) .unwrap_or_default() .as_secs() as i64 - 10 * 24 * 60 * 60; // 10 days ago let recent_babbage = std::time::SystemTime::now() .duration_since(std::time::UNIX_EPOCH) .unwrap_or_default() .as_secs() as i64 - 30 * 24 * 60 * 60; // 30 days ago // …then use them as created_at and assert against the same variables. ```

toolUseResult.stdout: **Hardcoded timestamps will cause test failures** `list_includes_created_at_in_response` uses absolute timestamps for two models that are then queried through `service.list()`, which now applies the new 1-year legacy filter: - `1_770_000_000` ≈ **Feb 2026** → `gpt-5.3` will be filtered out around **February 2027** - `1_760_000_000` ≈ **Oct 2025** → `babbage-002` will be filtered out around **October 2026** When `babbage-002` ages past the cutoff, both `assert_eq!(arr.len(), 3)` (line 11313) and the `.find(...).unwrap()` for `babbage-002` will panic. The `gpt-5.3` lookups fail roughly four months later. The new tests `list_hides_legacy_models_by_default` correctly avoid this by computing timestamps relative to `SystemTime::now()`. The same approach should be used here: ```rust // Instead of hardcoded absolute timestamps: let recent_gpt = std::time::SystemTime::now() .duration_since(std::time::UNIX_EPOCH) .unwrap_or_default() .as_secs() as i64 - 10 * 24 * 60 * 60; // 10 days ago let recent_babbage = std::time::SystemTime::now() .duration_since(std::time::UNIX_EPOCH) .unwrap_or_default() .as_secs() as i64 - 30 * 24 * 60 * 60; // 30 days ago // …then use them as created_at and assert against the same variables. ```
